era_context: The Roman Empire under Hadrian (r. 117–138 CE) at its territorial zenith. The Bar Kokhba Revolt in Judaea has recently concluded (132–135 CE). The Antonine Plague has not yet begun. China is in the Eastern Han dynasty under Emperor Andi (r. 106–125 CE) or his successors; the empire is stable but facing increasing eunuch influence and factional court politics. The Kushan Empire dominates Central Asia and the Silk Road trade routes.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)
Mesoamerica (no written chronology synchronized to CE calendar)

events.title: Bar Kokhba Revolt concludes; Jerusalem destroyed and rebuilt as Aelia Capitolina
events.description: The Bar Kokhba Revolt, which began in 132 CE, is suppressed by Roman forces under Sextus Julius Severus. The final stronghold at Betar falls in 135 CE. Jerusalem is razed and rebuilt as a pagan city, Aelia Capitolina, with a temple to Jupiter Capitolinus erected on the site of the Jewish Temple. Jews are expelled from Jerusalem and surrounding areas. This marks the end of Jewish political autonomy in the region for nearly two millennia and catalyzes the diaspora.
key_figures: Simon bar Kokhba (Jewish rebel leader, died in battle)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Betar and Jerusalem excavations
Bar Kokhba letters (discovered in Judean Desert caves)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Betar and suppression of the revolt in 135 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. Archaeological evidence from the site and contemporary letters confirm the dating.
cross_references: Leads to diaspora and Jewish settlement patterns in subsequent centuries

events.title: Hadrian's Wall construction continues in Britannia
events.description: Construction of Hadrian's Wall, begun around 122 CE, continues under Hadrian's direct oversight. By 135 CE, the wall is substantially complete, stretching approximately 80 Roman miles (117 km) across northern Britain from the Solway Firth to the Tyne estuary. The wall serves as a frontier fortification and customs barrier, marking the northern limit of direct Roman control in Britain. Forts, milecastles, and turrets are garrisoned with auxiliary troops.
key_figures: Hadrian (Roman Emperor, visited Britain c. 122 CE)
Aulus Platorius Nepos (Roman governor of Britain, c. 122–126 CE)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the wall itself, forts, and inscriptions
Inscriptions on stones from the wall

events.certainty_note: The wall's construction is well-documented archaeologically and epigraphically. The dating of major phases is secure, though the exact completion date is debated (sometime between 130–140 CE).
cross_references: Part of broader Roman frontier consolidation under Hadrian
Precedes the Antonine Wall construction (c. 142 CE)

events.title: Eastern Han court politics: eunuch influence increases under Emperor Andi's successors
events.description: Following the death of Emperor Andi in 125 CE, the Eastern Han court becomes increasingly dominated by eunuch factions and the families of imperial consorts. By 135 CE, eunuchs hold significant administrative and military power, a trend that will accelerate throughout the 2nd century and contribute to the dynasty's eventual collapse. The imperial succession is contested and unstable, with child emperors and regents. This period marks the beginning of the institutional decline that characterizes the late Eastern Han.
key_figures: Liang Ji (powerful eunuch and general, d. 159 CE)
Empress Liang (regent, mother of Emperor Shun)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Hanshu (History of the Han), compiled earlier but covering this period
Later dynastic histories (Hou Hanshu, compiled 5th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of eunuch ascendancy in the mid-2nd century is well-established. The specific year 135 CE is assigned by convention as a marker within this longer process; the exact chronology of individual court intrigues is less certain.
cross_references: Part of the decline of the Eastern Han dynasty (leads to collapse in 220 CE)
Contemporary with Roman political stability under Hadrian (contrast)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Bar Kokhba Revolt's end is traditionally placed at 135 CE, but some scholars argue for 134 CE based on numismatic evidence (the last coins minted by the rebels). The exact year of Betar's fall remains debated by a margin of ±1 year. Additionally, the significance of the Bar Kokhba Revolt as a watershed moment in Jewish history is sometimes overstated in popular accounts; Jewish communities continued to exist in Judaea and the diaspora was already well-established before 135 CE.
historiographic_note: The year 135 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire and Judaea, with multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent. For China, the historical record is rich but filtered through later compilations; contemporary records from 135 CE itself are sparse. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no synchronized written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Conclusion of the Bar Kokhba Revolt that began in 132 CE
Second major Jewish-Roman conflict, following the First Jewish-Roman War (66–70 CE)
Continuation of Hadrian's Wall construction begun c. 122 CE
Eunuch dominance contributes to the collapse of the Eastern Han dynasty in 220 CE
